content.problemStatement: Q2) A . Explain pointer arithmetic with suitable examples ?  ---4 Marks.
content.inputFormat: The problem does not require any input.
content.outputFormat: Print a concise explanation of pointer arithmetic in C/C++, including how adding an integer to a pointer moves it by that many elements, and examples demonstrating pointer increment, array indexing via pointers, and pointer subtraction.

sampleTestCases.output:
A pointer to an int points to a memory location holding an int. Adding 1 to the pointer moves it to the next int (address increases by sizeof(int)). Example:
int arr[3] = {5,10,15};
int *p = arr; // points to arr[0]
printf("%d", *(p+2)); // prints 15
p++; // now points to arr[1]
printf("%d", *p); // prints 10
Pointer subtraction gives the number of elements between two pointers of the same array.
sampleTestCases.explanation: Demonstrates pointer increment, indexing via pointer arithmetic, and subtraction.

solution.approach: Pointer arithmetic works on the type of the object the pointer points to. Adding an integer k to a pointer p of type T* yields a pointer to the element k positions after p (p + k). Similarly, subtracting yields a pointer k positions before. The difference between two pointers to elements of the same array gives the number of elements between them. Examples: int a[5] = {10,20,30,40,50}; int *p = a; // points to a[0] p++; // now points to a[1] *(p+2) == a[2] // 30 int *q = &a[4]; int diff = q - p; // number of elements between q and p. These operations are scaled by sizeof(T).
